sections.heading: Overview and Presentation
sections.content: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare and aggressive primary cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasm. It originates from Merkel cells, which are mechanoreceptor cells located in the basal layer of the epidermis and are thought to derive from the ectoderm. MCC is characterized by small, round, blue cells with neuroendocrine features, often displaying high mitotic activity and dense-core granules on electron microscopy. Anatomically, MCC arises in the skin, most commonly on sun-exposed areas such as the head and neck (approximately 50% of cases) and extremities. However, it can occur at virtually any cutaneous site. The disease is clinically significant due to its rapid growth potential and high propensity for early lymphatic and hematogenous metastasis. The primary tumor often presents as a painless, firm, red-to-violaceous nodule or plaque, which may be mistaken clinically for a benign cyst or other skin lesion.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Markers
sections.content: Histopathological confirmation requires immunohistochemistry (IHC) to distinguish MCC from cutaneous simulants and metastatic tumors, with key diagnostic markers including positive cytokeratin 20 (CK20) staining in a characteristic membranous and/or paranuclear dot-like pattern and negative thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF-1) expression.

sections.heading: TNM Staging
sections.content: The disease is staged using the AJCC 8th edition TNM system, which categorizes tumors based on primary tumor size and depth of invasion, regional lymph node involvement, and distant metastasis.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: MCC is associated with a poor prognosis, with 5-year survival rates ranging from 41% to 77% depending on stage at presentation, underscoring its status as one of the most lethal skin cancers, with a mortality rate exceeding that of melanoma.
